text: The police department could do stings like this semi regularly for a very low budget, make some well publicized arrests, and effectively eliminate porch piracy
thread: The police department could do stings like this semi regularly for a very low budget, make some well publicized arrests, and effectively eliminate porch piracy

porch pirates are so common because the risk:reward ratio of this crime is amazing. there is practically zero risk. 

1) it's almost impossible to get caught by passerby, u can just act like u live there 2) police departments have bigger fish to fry

effectively ur worst case is
reply:
tbh porch piracy feels like a hack in the social contract. it only works because everyone is so checked out they don’t notice a stranger on their porch.
imagine even ten neighbors actually looking out for each other. the risk would shoot up overnight.

text: there's trend in tender romance movies (You've Got Mail, One Day, Sleepless in Seattle) where Boy and Girl have long simmering emotional intimacy and sexual tension while one or both is in arid relationship with someone Nice But Uninspiring. https://t.co/rYmSQ4vjpg
thread: there's trend in tender romance movies (You've Got Mail, One Day, Sleepless in Seattle) where Boy and Girl have long simmering emotional intimacy and sexual tension while one or both is in arid relationship with someone Nice But Uninspiring. https://t.co/rYmSQ4vjpg

these movies are all very popular, and I guess i'm surprised more people don't object to the emotional affair. 

I wonder what role the emotional affair plays in the plot.  Is it to make things more realistic, to contrast True Love with Simply Okay Love instead of loneliness?
reply:
it’s bc most people’s real lives are a “Nice But Uninspiring” relationship, so emotional affairs in fiction feel like a lifeline, like permission to want more
the movies would bomb if they were just about two single people meeting at a coffee shop. realism gets to piggyback off wish fulfillment this way
